title: Source Library for Palestine
subtitle: A reading map for primary texts, scholarship, law, and responsible reporting
summary: There is no single neutral source that can answer every question about Palestine. This draft reading map explains what different source types can and cannot establish, so readers can build a careful and balanced research process.

sections.title: In Plain Words
sections.body:
Use more than one type of source. Primary documents show what an institution or person said; they do not automatically prove that every claim in the document is correct. Scholarly books can explain context; they still have arguments and limits. Reporting can document recent events; it must be dated and checked.

The strongest reading list tells you what each item is for instead of treating every link as equal evidence.

sections.title: Build a Balanced Source Set
sections.body:
Begin with primary texts in reliable editions: religious texts, legal documents, census records, archives, and official statements. Add peer-reviewed scholarship and books from established academic presses for historical context. For contemporary developments, prefer reporting that gives dates, named sources, corrections, and a clear distinction between verified facts and allegations.

Read more than one scholarly perspective, especially where historians disagree. A source's political or religious perspective should be named rather than hidden, but perspective alone does not decide whether a specific piece of evidence is sound.

sections.title: How to Use This Library
sections.body:
This draft list is an entry point, not a certification that every source is complete or impartial. Follow links to the original publisher where possible, retain publication details, and record the page or section supporting a claim. For a high-stakes legal, medical, safety, or current-affairs question, consult qualified professionals and the most recent primary material.

If a source cannot be checked, say so. Leaving a question open is more honest than filling the gap with certainty.

furtherReading.title: The Question of Palestine
furtherReading.author: United Nations
furtherReading.note: United Nations portal collecting documents and background material; primary institutional sources should be read with their authorship and mandate in mind.

furtherReading.title: The Palestine Question in Maps: 1878-2013
furtherReading.author: P. R. Kumaraswamy
furtherReading.note: A map-based historical reference; maps should be read with their dates, makers, and stated purposes.

furtherReading.title: Palestine and the Arab-Israeli Conflict: A History with Documents
furtherReading.author: Charles D. Smith
furtherReading.note: A widely assigned historical survey with documents; compare it with specialist works for particular periods.
